methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.2B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Base Power, a Series C climate and energy company founded by Zach Dell and Justin Lopas, has raised over $1.25B to date. The valuation analysis relies on two applicable triangulation methods anchored to a comp universe of three same-stage, same-sector private peers (KoBold Metals at $2.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M) plus one public comparable and eight additional private frontier-tech companies in climate and energy.

The stage-progression method applies a 1.7x multiplier to the $1.2B total capital raised proxy, implying a $2.1B valuation. The peer-median method identifies a $1.0B median valuation across the three direct comparables, with a range of $700M to $2.0B, reflecting meaningful dispersion in how the market values Series C energy infrastructure businesses. These two methods diverge notably, with the stage-progression approach implying higher multiples on cumulative capital than the peer set currently commands.

Synthesizing both approaches yields a triangulated median valuation of $1.6B, with a defensible range of $850M to $2.4B. The wide dispersion reflects two material uncertainties: the heterogeneity of business models and commercialization timelines within frontier energy, and the limited liquidity comparables for validation. The peer-median floor of $1.0B anchors conservatively to observable market pricing, while the stage-progression ceiling acknowledges capital intensity typical of this sector.
